text:
The most overrated kind of beauty, in my view, is the kind that has been optimized to be immediately and universally legible. Symmetry, smooth skin, clean proportion, the photogenic face, the sunset that looks exactly like every other sunset you were told to admire. None of these things are bad, and some are genuinely lovely, but they are overrated in the precise sense that we pay them more than they are worth. They cash in instantly. They require nothing of you. And because they ask for no effort, they also tend to give very little back over time. You see a flawless thing, register that it is flawless, and then there is almost nowhere else to go with it.

What gets crowded out by this is the beauty that only opens up on a second or third encounter, or that needs you to know something before it can move you. A weathered face is more interesting than a smooth one because it has kept a record. A piece of music that seems merely odd at first and then, after you live with it, reorganizes how you hear everything else is doing something a catchy melody never will. The beauty of a worn tool, a repaired thing, a landscape that is not conventionally scenic but that you have walked through enough times to love. These are not beautiful in spite of their imperfections so much as beautiful partly because of them, because the imperfection is where the particular history and the particular meaning attach.

I think the overrating happens for an understandable reason, which is that legible beauty is easy to agree about, easy to photograph, easy to sell, and increasingly easy to manufacture and to filter. So it spreads, and its very ubiquity makes it feel like the standard against which everything else is a falling-off. But sameness is corrosive to beauty. The thousandth perfect sunset is less moving than the first, whereas a strange and difficult thing can actually deepen with familiarity rather than wearing out. If I had to compress it: the most overrated beauty is the sort you can fully take in at a glance and never need to return to, because the experiences I would actually call beautiful are nearly all ones that rewarded coming back.
